reasoning: While President Trump has generated considerable headlines in early 2025 by questioning the validity of the 1908 U.S.-Canada border treaty and floating the idea of Canada becoming the "51st state," the likelihood of the U.S. actually acquiring Canadian territory by the end of 2025 is negligible. Historically, land acquisitions between stable democratic nations require mutual consent, usually codified in treaties after years of complex negotiation. Canada's leadership (both former PM Justin Trudeau and new PM Mark Carney) has firmly and categorically rejected any such boundary revisions or annexation, with Carney calling the U.S. "a country we can no longer trust." Furthermore, Trump has explicitly ruled out the use of military force, citing "economic force" instead. Any attempt at economic coercion would fail to swiftly override Canadian sovereignty within a 9-month timeframe given Canada's constitutional safeguards and intense political opposition. Even if Trump pursues a unilateral executive order claiming territory, such an action would lack mutual recognition or physical control, failing the standard of "acquisition" recognized by "a consensus of credible reporting." The Polymarket odds hovering around 9% largely reflect a premium on Trump's unpredictable rhetoric rather than a realistic legal or diplomatic pathway for territorial expansion in 2025. Therefore, the probability is near zero.
